skill.name: simpy
skill.description: Build Python discrete-event simulations with SimPy for queues, shared resources, process timing, monitoring, and real-time behavior.
skill.summary: Create and analyze SimPy simulations for resource-constrained systems.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and crypto matches, but review found these are mostly Markdown backticks, SimPy Environment references, and ordinary simulation examples. No prompt injection, external command execution, credential access, or network client behavior was found. The only confirmed side effect is local CSV file writing for simulation metrics, so the skill is low risk and publishable with a filename caution.

low_findings.title: Caller-Controlled CSV File Write
low_findings.description: The ResourceMonitor.export_csv helper writes simulation metrics to a filename supplied by the caller. This is expected monitoring behavior, but untrusted filenames could overwrite local files if a user passes a sensitive path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and semantically confirmed. Risk is limited because the skill does not automatically execute the helper and there is no evidence of secret access or network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Network Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings correspond to Markdown code fences and inline SimPy examples, not shell execution. The reported network findings describe network simulation scenarios and do not show HTTP clients or outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged lines and searched for command execution and HTTP client APIs. The evidence shows Python simulation examples, not subprocess use, shell calls, or network clients.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Export Helper
dangerous_patterns.description: Simulation monitoring data can be exported to CSV with open(filename, write mode). This is a normal utility pattern but should be used with trusted output paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write path is visible in code and has a clear benign purpose. The concern is limited to local file overwrite risk when a caller chooses an unsafe filename.

content.value_statement: Complex operations often need testing before teams change real systems. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code build SimPy models, monitor results, and explain tradeoffs.
actual_capabilities: Explains SimPy environments, processes, events, resources, containers, and stores.
Provides queue and customer-server simulation templates with configurable parameters.
Shows monitoring patterns for wait times, utilization, queue length, and event traces.
Demonstrates real-time simulation setup with SimPy RealtimeEnvironment.
Includes resource monitor utilities that can print reports and export simulation metrics to CSV.
limitations: Does not install SimPy or manage Python environments for the user.
Focuses on discrete-event simulation, not continuous differential equation models.
Example models require validation before operational or safety-critical use.
CSV export writes to local paths supplied by the caller.

use_cases.title: Plan Service Capacity
use_cases.description: Model customer arrivals, service times, queue length, and utilization before changing staffing or server counts.

use_cases.title: Prototype Manufacturing Flow
use_cases.description: Represent machines, buffers, priorities, and interruptions to compare process designs under controlled assumptions.

use_cases.title: Teach Simulation Concepts
use_cases.description: Use small examples to explain events, process synchronization, resources, and monitoring in Python.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Queue Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple SimPy customer-server queue. Use one resource, random arrivals, random service times, and a short explanation of each part.

prompt_templates.title: Add Resource Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend my SimPy model to track average wait time, maximum queue length, and resource utilization. Explain where the measurements are recorded.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Capacity Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a SimPy experiment that compares two, three, and four servers. Report throughput, wait time, and utilization for each option.

prompt_templates.title: Model Advanced Interactions
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an advanced SimPy model with priority resources, interruptions, and optional real-time execution. Include validation checks and monitoring outputs.

output_examples.input: Create a small emergency department queue simulation.
output_examples.output: A SimPy model outline with patient arrival logic, nurse and doctor resources, treatment time assumptions, and a metrics summary for waits and utilization.

output_examples.input: Explain why my resource utilization is above target.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis that reviews arrival rate, service duration, resource capacity, queue growth, and suggested experiments to test staffing changes.

output_examples.input: Add monitoring to a production-line simulation.
output_examples.output: A monitoring plan that records buffer levels, machine use, queue length, and final summary statistics without changing the process rules.

best_practices: Set random seeds and document time units before comparing scenarios.
Validate simple cases against hand calculations before using complex models.
Collect metrics during the simulation, then summarize after the run completes.
anti_patterns: Using SimPy for continuous physics or differential equation models.
Treating example output as a business decision without sensitivity analysis.
Writing CSV exports to sensitive or shared paths without checking the filename.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps build SimPy discrete-event simulations for queues, resources, events, monitoring, and real-time examples.

faq.question: Does it run simulations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and Python templates that the user can run in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it model manufacturing systems?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples cover resources, stores, containers, preemption, monitoring, and capacity comparisons.

faq.question: Can it model continuous systems?
faq.answer: Not directly. It is intended for discrete events, so continuous models need tools such as SciPy solvers.

faq.question: What outputs can I expect?
faq.answer: Typical outputs include model structure, Python simulation templates, monitoring plans, and plain-language analysis summaries.

faq.question: Are there security considerations?
faq.answer: The included scripts are local simulation utilities. CSV export writes to caller-provided paths, so filenames should be reviewed.
